title: How to Rent a Boat in Turkey and Cruise the Turquoise Coast
tags: boat rental, yacht charter, turkey travel, sailing, turquoise coast
topic: How to rent a boat in Turkey and cruise the Turquoise Coast
html:
The Turquoise Coast is the kind of place that ruins you for ordinary holidays. Pine forests tumble straight into water so clear you can count the pebbles three metres down, hidden coves open up behind headlands with no road access, and ancient ruins sit half-submerged where Lycian cities slipped into the sea centuries ago. The honest truth is that you only see the best of it from the water. Hotels cluster around the busy resorts, but the quiet bays, the secret swimming spots and the tiny fishing harbours belong to people on boats. This is a practical guide to renting one and doing the coast properly.

Why a boat beats a bus tour here

Turkey's southwest coast, running roughly from Bodrum down through Fethiye, Gocek and on towards Antalya, is a sailor's paradise that the rest of the world is slowly catching on to. The water is warm from May to October, the winds are steady but forgiving, and there are hundreds of anchorages packed into a relatively short stretch of coast. You can wake up in a different bay every morning, swim before breakfast, and never queue for a sunlounger. When you want to rent a boat for this trip, you are really buying freedom: the freedom to drop anchor wherever the water looks good and stay as long as you like.

Compare that to the land route. The coast road is beautiful but winding, parking near the good beaches is a nightmare in August, and the genuinely special coves simply cannot be reached on foot. A boat solves all of it at once.

Choosing the right type of boat

The biggest decision is what kind of vessel suits your group and your appetite for hands-on sailing. Broadly you have three options:

  • Bareboat charter: you skipper it yourself. Cheapest per day, total privacy, but you need a recognised sailing qualification and real confidence handling a yacht.
  • Skippered charter: a professional captain handles the boat while you relax. The sweet spot for most travellers, and the captain doubles as a local guide who knows every hidden bay.
  • Gulet cruise: the classic Turkish wooden motor-sailer, usually with a small crew and cook. Slow, comfortable and very social, ideal for families or groups who want to be looked after.

If you have never sailed before, do not let that stop you. A skippered yacht or a gulet means you get the full experience with none of the stress. Take a little time to browse boats across these categories before you commit, because the difference in atmosphere between a sleek catamaran and a traditional gulet is enormous.

A sample week on the water

Most people base themselves out of Fethiye or Gocek, which sit at the heart of the best cruising grounds. A relaxed seven-day loop might look like this:

  1. Day one: collect the boat in Gocek, provision up, and motor out to a quiet first-night anchorage in the Gocek islands.
  2. Days two and three: work your way into Fethiye Bay, stopping at Cleopatra's Bath and the green water of Wall Bay.
  3. Day four: head for Butterfly Valley and the dramatic cliffs near Oludeniz, swimming off the back of the boat in deep blue water.
  4. Days five and six: push towards the Twelve Islands, where the swimming and snorkelling are some of the finest anywhere.
  5. Day seven: a gentle sail back to base, with one last lunch stop in a sheltered cove.

None of this is rushed. The whole point is to slow down to the speed of the sea.

Practical tips before you book

A few things worth knowing. The Meltemi wind can pick up in the afternoons during high summer, so plan to be settled in your anchorage by mid-afternoon. Bring cash for the small waterside restaurants and the harbour fees in places like Kekova. Pack reef shoes, a good hat and far more sunscreen than you think you need. And book earlier than feels necessary: the best boats for July and August are often spoken for by spring. When you are ready to hire a boat, having dates and a rough route in mind makes the whole process far quicker.

The Turquoise Coast rewards people who keep their plans loose. The best day of the trip is almost always the unscheduled one, when the captain says he knows a bay that is not on any map.

Booking it without the hassle

The old way of arranging a charter meant phoning a dozen local agents and comparing scrappy PDFs. It is far simpler now to compare verified listings, owners and skippers in one place. A marketplace lets you filter by size, price, crew and dates, read genuine reviews, and message the owner directly before you pay. If you own a vessel on the coast yourself, the same platform lets you list your boat and reach travellers from all over the world during the long Turkish season.

The Turquoise Coast has been drawing sailors for thousands of years, and once you have anchored in your first silent cove with nothing but cicadas and clear water for company, you understand exactly why. Sort the boat, point the bow south, and let the coast do the rest.
